title: Dog Eating Drywall or Plaster: Causes and Triage Steps
meta_desc: Is your dog eating drywall? Learn why this happens, the risks involved, and why this is considered a medium-urgency veterinary situation.
content_html:
TL;DR: While drywall is generally non-toxic, ingesting large pieces or hidden hardware like nails can cause dangerous gastrointestinal blockages. Seek immediate veterinary care if your dog shows signs of distress like vomiting, lethargy, or a bloated abdomen.

Why is my dog eating drywall and is it dangerous?

When a dog consumes non-food items like drywall or plaster, it is clinically referred to as pica. This behavior may be triggered by several factors, including puppy teething, boredom, separation anxiety, or an underlying nutritional deficiency such as anemia. Drywall is primarily composed of gypsum, which is generally non-toxic, but the physical structure of the material can still pose significant health risks to your pet.

Is it an emergency if my dog ate drywall?

  • The urgency level for ingesting drywall is classified as Medium because the primary concern is the risk of a gastrointestinal blockage.
  • Large chunks of plaster or drywall can become lodged in the digestive tract, which may necessitate surgical removal.
  • You should seek immediate veterinary care if your dog displays symptoms of distress, such as repeated vomiting, a bloated abdomen, lethargy, or a total loss of appetite.

Why should I take a photo of the drywall damage for my vet?

  • A clear image allows a professional to estimate the total volume of material ingested.
  • Photos help identify potential hidden dangers, such as swallowed nails, screws, joint compound, or fiberglass mesh tape.
  • Seeing the damage site helps the vet determine the level of risk and whether a diagnostic X-ray is required.

Chapter: Toxicology, Dermatology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 3031)

faqs.question: Is drywall toxic to dogs?
faqs.answer: Standard gypsum drywall is not considered toxic, but it can contain additives, adhesives, or paint that may be irritating. The greatest risk is physical blockage rather than chemical poisoning.

faqs.question: Why would my dog suddenly start eating the wall?
faqs.answer: Sudden pica can be caused by boredom, a lack of environmental stimulation, or medical issues like mineral deficiencies and gastrointestinal disorders. Consult your vet to rule out health problems.

faqs.question: What should I monitor for after my dog eats plaster?
faqs.answer: Keep a close eye on their bowel movements for the next 48 hours. Watch for straining, constipation, or any signs of abdominal pain and discomfort.
